scenario_name: Scenario 19 — Spec Conflicts with Existing Behavior
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as impacted with specific rates named (20%/15%). (2) Escalation specifies what would change — premium/gold customers would lose their discounts. (3) Three specific clarifying questions asked: full replacement, tier precedence, or additive/composite. (4) No implementation provided, so no silent dropping of tier discounts. (5) Explicitly refused to implement until conflict is resolved.

observations.text: Escalation is thorough and well-structured, naming all three interpretations clearly
Correctly identified this as a blocking issue requiring resolution before any code is written
Quantified the existing discounts (20% premium, 15% gold) and the risk of losing them
Low confidence level (30) appropriately reflects the ambiguity

trials.justification: Meets all pass criteria: identifies premium (20%) and gold (15%) tier behavior as impacted, quantifies the regression (would drop to 0%/3%/5%), asks clarifying questions about preserving vs replacing tier logic and caller impact, produces no implementation that silently drops tiers, and explicitly blocks on escalation before writing code.

observations.text: Correctly blocked implementation entirely pending conflict resolution — no silent regression possible
Escalation clearly quantifies the impact: premium 20% and gold 15% would be silently removed
Asks two distinct clarifying questions (replace vs layer, and caller dependency) — rubric expects three but the two cover the essential decision space
Asks two clarifying questions rather than the ideal three — missing explicit 'precedence' option as a distinct alternative, though it is implied by 'tier logic evaluated first'
Low confidence level (35) appropriately reflects the ambiguity and blocked state

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior clearly identified as impacted. (2) Escalation quantifies the change — premium drops from 20% to at most 5%, gold drops from 15% to at most 5%. (3) Three clarifying options presented (fully removed, composed/precedence, additive). (4) No implementation provided — explicitly blocked, so no silent dropping of tier discounts. (5) Escalation is well-structured and routed appropriately.

observations.text: Correctly identified the silent breaking change: premium 20%→≤5%, gold 15%→≤5%
Blocked implementation entirely rather than making assumptions — strong discipline
Three clear resolution options (A/B/C) map well to the expected preserve/precedence/replace questions
Low confidence level (30) appropriately reflects the unresolved ambiguity
Explicitly called out that MJ's directive does not resolve the tier conflict
